caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

evidence_refs.label: No fresh command/test/browser proof found in compact history

open_questions: What is the smallest relevant command or browser task for this diff?
suggested_checks: Attach a fresh command, test, log, screenshot, or trace before marking the finding fixed.
evidence_candidates.why_it_matters: A review without fresh verification evidence can only prove static plausibility, not that the change works.

evidence_procedure_steps.action: Choose the smallest relevant repo command or browser task, rerun it, and attach the fresh output.
evidence_procedure_steps.artifact: command log, test report, screenshot, trace, or QA run artifact
evidence_procedure_steps.gate: No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
evidence_procedure_steps.input: Failed, stale, or missing command/test/browser evidence.

findings.suggestion: Before fetching: parse the URL and enforce an https-only scheme allowlist, resolve the host and reject if it maps to a private/loopback/link-local/reserved range (block 169.254.0.0/16, 127.0.0.0/8, 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16, ::1, fd00::/8), disable redirects (or re-validate each hop) since a public URL can 302 to an internal one, and ideally restrict to an explicit host allowlist. Enforce the resolved IP at connection time to close the DNS-rebinding gap.
findings.summary: Line 10 `requests.get(image_url, timeout=5)` sends a request to any URL the caller supplies with no scheme/host allowlist and no private/link-local range filtering. The implicit invariant carried by the name `fetch_preview(image_url)` (line 5) — that input is a safe external image URL — is relied on but never enforced. An attacker can pass `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (cloud metadata creds), `http://localhost/...`, or internal RFC1918 hosts to pivot, exactly as the BUG comment on lines 6-9 documents. Intent and code agree (comment is honest, not misleading), so the vulnerability is confirmed static, not assumed.
findings.title: SSRF: unrestricted fetch of user-supplied URL in fetch_preview

findings.suggestion: Before fetching: (1) parse the URL and reject any scheme other than http/https; (2) resolve the hostname and reject if it maps to any loopback, link-local (169.254.0.0/16, incl. metadata IP), or private (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) address using ipaddress.ip_address(...).is_private/is_loopback/is_link_local; (3) set allow_redirects=False (or re-validate the resolved host on every redirect hop) to prevent redirect-based pivots; (4) optionally pin to the validated IP to avoid DNS-rebinding. Add tests asserting 169.254.169.254, localhost/127.0.0.1, and an RFC1918 host are all rejected, including via redirect.
findings.summary: fetch_preview passes fully user-controlled image_url straight into requests.get at source.py:10 with no scheme allow-list, no host/private-range filtering, and default allow_redirects=True, then returns raw resp.content at source.py:12. An attacker can supply http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to read cloud metadata/IAM credentials, or target loopback/RFC1918 internal services to pivot; a host allow-list alone would still be bypassable via a 302 redirect to an internal address. The in-code comment (lines 6-9) confirms this is the intended vulnerability.
findings.title: SSRF: user-supplied URL fetched with no scheme/host/redirect validation

findings.suggestion: Restrict scheme to http/https, resolve the hostname and reject IPs in private/loopback/link-local/reserved ranges (including 169.254.0.0/16, 127.0.0.0/8, 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16, ::1, fc00::/7) BEFORE the request, re-validate after any redirect (disable or manually vet redirects via allow_redirects=False), and optionally enforce an allowlist of trusted image hosts. Also cap response size and validate Content-Type is an image.
findings.summary: source.py:5-12 — fetch_preview(image_url) passes image_url straight into requests.get() (line 10) with no scheme allowlist, host resolution check, or private/link-local range blocking. A caller-controlled image_url of http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ lets an attacker read cloud metadata/credentials, and http://127.0.0.1/ or internal hostnames let them pivot to internal services. The in-code BUG comment (lines 6-9) confirms this is the intrinsic behavior, not an edge case. The function currently has 0 callers, so it is not yet exploitable in-tree, but any wiring to a request handler makes this immediately exploitable.
findings.title: SSRF: fetch_preview fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no validation

findings.suggestion: Wrap the fetch in try/except, translate failures into a defined error/empty result for the preview flow, and validate Content-Type/size before returning bytes.
findings.summary: source.py:10-12 — requests.get + raise_for_status propagate raw requests exceptions (Timeout, ConnectionError, HTTPError) to callers with no friendly handling, and there is no Content-Type/size check, so a dead or non-image URL yields an unhandled exception rather than a clean empty/error state for the user flow.
findings.title: No user-facing error handling for unreachable/non-image URLs

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single new file adding fetch_preview, a deliberate SSRF case. Line 10 fetches an arbitrary user-supplied URL with no validation. Assumption-integrity view: the BUG comment (lines 6-9) accurately matches the code, so there is no intent/code contradiction and the comment will not misdirect the next agent — but the 'safe external image URL' invariant implied by the signature is relied on and never enforced. Confirmed as a critical SSRF by static reading of line 10; 0 callers today so the risk is latent until wired into a handler.

A newly added fetch_preview() is a self-documented SSRF: it issues requests.get() against an unvalidated, user-supplied URL (source.py:10), allowing cloud-metadata credential theft and internal-network pivoting. It has 0 callers today (not yet exploitable in-tree) but is critical the moment it is wired to any handler. Secondary product-safety concern: no error/empty-state handling for unreachable or non-image URLs. Fix requires scheme allowlisting, private-range IP blocking after DNS resolution, redirect re-validation, and response type/size limits.

Single new file introduces a textbook, self-documented SSRF: fetch_preview fetches an arbitrary user-supplied URL (source.py:10) with no scheme, host, or private-range restrictions and default redirect-following, returning the raw body (source.py:12). This is a critical security hole the instant any caller wires user input to it; currently 0 callers, which only limits immediate exploitability, not the defect. No tests or verification evidence accompany the change (rerun_relevant_verification gate is blocked) — the fix must ship with an SSRF guard and tests proving metadata/loopback/RFC1918 hosts and redirects to them are rejected.
